That 'random customer' who suggested the display case location was you wearing a disguise.
You took the graveyard shift janitor job just to learn the security patterns.
The maintenance logs show you disabled just one camera at 3:14 AM - the one covering that case.
You replaced the robot with a replica so the theft wouldn't be noticed until you were gone.
But you forgot the replicas all have two red hands. That's what tipped us off.
You wanted to get caught eventually, didn't you? Leaving all these deliberate clues.
The note in the display case - 'For Grandpa Joe' - that was your signature.
You knew the store owner would recognize that name from the original police report.
This was never about the toy - it was about proving the theft happened.
Your grandfather died thinking people believed he lied about the robbery.
You needed the world to know his story was true after all these years.
That's why you left the security audio on - to record your confession.

I found the old newspaper clipping in your jacket pocket - the one about my grandfather's dismissal from the force.

The timing was too perfect - finding it just after I mentioned the 1950s case files.
Tell me about the blueprints in your apartment. Were those part of the original evidence?

You studied them obsessively these past months. Those weren't just research notes, were they?
The robot's original box was in your closet too - but empty. Where's the actual toy now?
You never intended to keep it. This was always about recreating the original crime scene.

You needed official police documentation to clear your grandfather's name properly.

Those weren't your footsteps on the audio. You hired someone to wear period-correct shoes, didn't you?
The way the glass broke - identical to the 1953 report. Too identical to be coincidence.

The security guard you paid to look away - he's the grandson of the original night watchman, isn't he?
Every detail was designed to mirror the original crime exactly. But why stage it at 3:15 AM again?
The moon angle. That was part of your grandfather's original alibi that no one believed.
The shadow patterns in your recreation video match his testimony perfectly. That's your smoking gun.
You even used period-accurate glass to ensure the breakage pattern would forensically match.
This wasn't just about clearing his name - it was about proving his observational skills were flawless all along.

You even replicated the temperature differential that caused the glass to contract just right.

You knew only a perfect recreation would force them to reopen the original case files.
The broken glass patterns were your grandfather's undocumented forensic signature.
The toy wasn't stolen at all - it was returned to its rightful owner after 70 years.
That's why you left the insurance papers proving your grandfather bought it legally in 1952.
This was never about theft - it was about restoring an old man's legacy through meticulous detective work.

I was out on my boat that night, like I always am during the full moon. The water was calm, but something felt off.
Around midnight, I saw a bright light near the old lighthouse. It wasn’t the usual beam—it was flickering, almost like a distress signal.
I thought maybe a tourist got lost, so I started heading that way. But then the light just... vanished.
That’s when I noticed the figure on the cliffs. Tall, dressed all in black, just standing there staring at the water.
I called out, but they didn’t respond. Then they turned and walked toward the lighthouse like they were in a trance.
I’ve lived here 30 years, detective. I know every face in town. That wasn’t anyone from around here.
The weirdest part? The tide was coming in, but their footprints... they just stopped halfway down the beach.
I tried to radio the coast guard, but my equipment was all static. Like something was interfering with it.
When I finally got close enough to shore, the figure was gone. No boat, no tracks leading away, nothing.
That’s when I heard the scream. Came from the direction of the caves beneath the lighthouse.
It wasn’t human, detective. More like... metal scraping on metal, but somehow forming words.
I rushed back to town to report it, but by the time anyone believed me, morning had come.
The search party found some torn fabric on the rocks—dark blue, like the missing girl’s jacket.
But here’s what they didn’t find: any sign that someone had climbed those cliffs recently.
The old timers say the lighthouse has been empty since ‘87. Power’s been cut for decades.
Yet that night? Every window was lit up like a Christmas tree.
Next morning, I went back to check. Place was dark again, locked tight with rusted chains.
Found something else too—a perfect circle of scorched sand where the figure had been standing.

But I swear on my boat’s name—every word’s true. That girl didn’t just disappear. Something took her.

There was no beam sweeping the water like a proper lighthouse should. Just steady glowing from every window.

Doctor, I've been experiencing more frequent migraines lately and need to discuss my medication.
I've been on propranolol for about two years now, which was working well at first.
But in the last three months, my migraine frequency has increased from about 2 to 5 episodes per month.
The headaches are just as intense as before, sometimes even more severe.
I'm still taking my current dose of 80mg daily as prescribed, but it doesn't seem as effective anymore.
The migraines usually start with visual disturbances, then progress to throbbing pain on one side of my head.
They typically last between 4 to 8 hours if I don't take my rescue medication right away.
I've noticed they tend to occur more often during stressful periods at work.
I'm also experiencing more sensitivity to light and sound during these episodes compared to before.
I sometimes have to take two or three days off work when a really bad migraine hits.
I'm concerned about whether I might need a different preventive medication or a dosage adjustment.
My current rescue medication is sumatriptan, which helps but doesn't always completely relieve the pain.
I've been tracking my migraines in a diary as you suggested at my last appointment.
I can show you the patterns I've noticed over these past few months.
I'm also wondering if there could be other factors contributing to this increase in frequency.
I haven't made any major lifestyle changes recently that would explain it.
My sleep schedule is consistent, and I've been careful about staying hydrated.
